question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Does the liquidate() function's NotLiquidatable check (Midnight.sol:620-624) evaluate postMaturityMode ? block.timestamp > market.maturity : originalDebt > maxDebt, where maxDebt is computed from oracle prices in the preceding while loop? If the oracle reverts, the while loop reverts before reaching the NotLiquidatable check, meaning the revert message is a generic EVM revert rather than NotLiquidatable. Can this difference in revert behavior be exploited by an attacker to confuse liquidation bots that check for specific revert reasons, causing them to incorrectly classify the position as non-liquidatable and stop retrying? Preconditions: oracle reverts; liquidation bot checks revert reason; bot stops retrying on non-NotLiquidatable reverts. Call sequence: (1) oracle reverts; (2) liquidation bot calls liquidate() → generic revert (not NotLiquidatable) → bot misclassifies as non-liquidatable → stops retrying. Invariant tested: liquidation bots must be able to distinguish oracle DoS from genuine non-liquidatable state. Scoped impact: indirect DoS via revert reason confusion. Proof idea: assert revert reason when oracle reverts vs when position is healthy.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / take] In take(), the seller health check at Midnight.sol:476 is: require(liquidationLocked(id, seller) || isHealthy(offer.market, id, seller)). If the seller has zero debt (sellerDebtIncrease > 0 but sellerPos.debt was 0 before), does isHealthy still call oracle (since debt > 0 after the state update), and does this mean a new borrower's first take as seller can be blocked by a reverting oracle even though they had no prior debt? Preconditions: seller has zero debt before take; take increases seller debt; oracle reverts. Call sequence: (1) seller has no debt; (2) taker calls take(sell_offer, units) → sellerDebtIncrease > 0 → sellerPos.debt updated to units; (3) isHealthy called with new debt > 0 → oracle called → reverts → take reverts. Invariant tested: take must succeed for new borrowers if their collateral is sufficient. Scoped impact: DoS on first-time borrowing via take when oracle reverts. Proof idea: assert take reverts for new seller with reverting oracle even with sufficient collateral.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower use multicall() (Midnight.sol:211-220) to atomically call supplyCollateral(market, j, 1, self) and then immediately have their position become unliquidatable in the same transaction, before any liquidator can react, by activating a collateral with a reverting oracle in a single atomic multicall? Preconditions: market has collateral j with reverting oracle; borrower has debt and is about to be liquidated; borrower calls multicall. Call sequence: (1) borrower calls multicall([supplyCollateral(j, 1, self), ...]) → collateral j activated atomically; (2) liquidator's pending liquidate() tx now fails because oracle for j reverts. Invariant tested: multicall must not allow atomic DoS setup that bypasses liquidation. Scoped impact: atomic DoS on liquidation via multicall. Proof idea: assert multicall(supplyCollateral) succeeds and subsequent liquidate fails.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] If a market has 128 collateral params (MAX_COLLATERALS=128) and a borrower activates 16 of them (MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER=16), does the liquidate() while loop iterate exactly the 16 activated collaterals (via collateralBitmap), or does it iterate all 128 params? Verify that the bitmap correctly limits oracle calls to activated collaterals only, and that there is no off-by-one in the msb/clearBit iteration that could cause an extra oracle call to an unactivated (and potentially reverting) collateral. Preconditions: market has 128 collaterals; borrower activates 16; some unactivated collaterals have reverting oracles. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates 16 collaterals; (2) liquidator calls liquidate() → while loop iterates collateralBitmap → only 16 oracle calls → succeeds. Invariant tested: oracle calls must be limited to activated collaterals only. Scoped impact: verify no spurious oracle calls to unactivated collaterals. Proof idea: assert liquidate calls oracle exactly countBits(collateralBitmap) times.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Does the take() function call isHealthy for the buyer (not just the seller), and if not, can a buyer with debt and a reverting oracle still successfully take buy offers without any oracle check? Verify that the oracle is only called for the seller's health check, and that buyers are never subject to oracle-dependent health checks during take. Preconditions: buyer has debt; buyer's collateral oracle reverts; buyer calls take(buy_offer). Call sequence: (1) buyer has debt and activated collateral with reverting oracle; (2) buyer calls take(buy_offer, ...) → buyerCreditIncrease computed → no isHealthy for buyer → oracle not called for buyer → take succeeds. Invariant tested: oracle DoS must not affect buyers in take. Scoped impact: verify buyer-side oracle independence in take. Proof idea: assert take(buy_offer) succeeds for buyer with reverting oracle.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] In the liquidate() function, after the while loop computes maxDebt and badDebt, the NotLiquidatable check uses originalDebt > maxDebt for normal mode. If the oracle returns an inflated price that makes maxDebt >= originalDebt (position appears healthy), does liquidate() revert with NotLiquidatable even though the position is actually unhealthy at the true price, and can a market creator deploy an oracle that returns inflated prices to prevent liquidation of undercollateralized positions? Preconditions: oracle returns inflated price P' > true price P; position is unhealthy at P but healthy at P'; liquidator calls liquidate(postMaturityMode=false). Call sequence: (1) oracle returns inflated P'; (2) maxDebt computed with P' >= originalDebt; (3) liquidate() reverts with NotLiquidatable. Invariant tested: liquidation must be possible for genuinely unhealthy positions. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via oracle price inflation. Proof idea: assert liquidate reverts with NotLiquidatable when oracle returns inflated price.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower who is the market creator set the oracle for their own collateral to a contract that returns a high price when called with low gas (making the position appear healthy) but reverts when called with normal gas, exploiting EIP-150 gas forwarding rules to make liquidation always fail while health checks in isHealthy (called with full gas) appear to pass? Preconditions: oracle uses gasleft() to determine behavior; liquidate() forwards less gas to oracle than isHealthy. Call sequence: (1) deploy GasCheckOracle: if gasleft() < threshold return high price else revert; (2) liquidator calls liquidate() → oracle called with gas < threshold → reverts; (3) isHealthy called with gas > threshold → returns high price → position appears healthy. Invariant tested: oracle behavior must be consistent between health checks and liquidation. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via gas-dependent oracle behavior. Proof idea: deploy GasCheckOracle, assert liquidate reverts while isHealthy returns healthy.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Does the liquidate() function's seizedAssets computation (Midnight.sol:650-653) use liquidatedCollatPrice which is set only if i == collateralIndex during the while loop (Midnight.sol:611), and if the collateralIndex is not in the borrower's collateralBitmap (i.e., the collateral is not activated), does liquidatedCollatPrice remain 0, causing division by zero when repaidUnits > 0? Preconditions: liquidator passes collateralIndex not in borrower's collateralBitmap; repaidUnits > 0. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates collateral 0 only; (2) liquidator calls liquidate(market, collateralIndex=1, 0, repaidUnits=1, ...) → while loop never sets liquidatedCollatPrice for index 1 → liquidatedCollatPrice=0 → seizedAssets = repaidUnits.mulDivDown(lif, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, 0) → division by zero. Invariant tested: liquidation must revert gracefully for invalid collateralIndex. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via unactivated collateralIndex with repaidUnits input. Proof idea: assert liquidate(collateralIndex not in bitmap, repaidUnits>0) reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Can a taker who is also the maker of a different offer in the same market use the liquidationLocked state (set during their own take callback) to call take() against a seller with a reverting oracle, since liquidationLocked(id, seller) is checked before isHealthy, and if the seller happens to be locked (e.g., seller is also in a take callback), the oracle is never called? Preconditions: seller is in a take callback (liquidationLocked=true); taker calls take against seller's offer during this window; seller's oracle reverts. Call sequence: (1) outer take sets liquidationLocked for seller; (2) during outer callback, taker calls inner take against seller → liquidationLocked=true → isHealthy skipped → oracle not called → inner take succeeds. Invariant tested: oracle-dependent health check must not be skippable via liquidation lock. Scoped impact: health check bypass during locked window. Proof idea: assert inner take during callback skips oracle and verify post-take health.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] In a market where all collateral oracles are functioning but one oracle returns a price that causes _collateral.mulDivDown(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE) to return a value that when multiplied by lltv/WAD causes maxDebt to exceed type(uint256).max in the accumulation (maxDebt += ...), does Solidity 0.8's checked arithmetic revert the liquidate() call, and can a market creator craft oracle prices to trigger this overflow only when the position is unhealthy (to block liquidation) but not when healthy (to pass isHealthy)? Preconditions: oracle returns P such that sum of collateral_i * P_i * lltv_i / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE / WAD overflows uint256 only for unhealthy positions. Call sequence: (1) oracle returns crafted P; (2) position is unhealthy; (3) liquidator calls liquidate() → maxDebt accumulation overflows → revert. Invariant tested: maxDebt computation must not overflow for any oracle price within uint256. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via targeted maxDebt overflow. Proof idea: construct (collateral, price, lltv) triple where maxDebt overflows, assert liquidate reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Does the liquidate() function call IOracle.price() before or after the NotBorrower check (Midnight.sol:596) and the liquidatorGate check (Midnight.sol:597-600)? If oracle is called before these checks, can a reverting oracle cause liquidate() to revert with a generic error even for non-borrowers or gated liquidators, masking the true revert reason and complicating bot logic? Preconditions: borrower has debt; oracle reverts; liquidatorGate would also revert. Call sequence: (1) oracle reverts; (2) liquidator calls liquidate() → NotBorrower check passes (debt>0) → liquidatorGate check passes → while loop → oracle reverts → generic revert. Invariant tested: revert ordering must be deterministic and informative. Scoped impact: revert reason masking complicates liquidation bot logic. Proof idea: assert revert reason ordering when multiple checks would fail.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] If a seller's collateral oracle returns a price that makes isHealthy return false (position appears unhealthy) even though the seller is actually healthy at the true price, does take() revert with SellerIsLiquidatable (Midnight.sol:476), blocking all take() calls against that seller's offers? Can a market creator deploy an oracle that deflates prices to make healthy sellers appear unhealthy, DoS-ing all their sell offers? Preconditions: oracle returns deflated price P' < true price P; seller is healthy at P but unhealthy at P'; seller has debt. Call sequence: (1) oracle returns deflated P'; (2) taker calls take(sell_offer) → isHealthy returns false → SellerIsLiquidatable revert. Invariant tested: take must succeed for genuinely healthy sellers. Scoped impact: DoS on take for all sell offers from sellers with deflated-price oracle. Proof idea: assert take reverts with SellerIsLiquidatable when oracle deflates price below health threshold.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower who has activated a collateral with a reverting oracle use the zero-collateral liquidation path (Midnight.sol:577-579: 'Liquidations with both 0 for seizedAssets and repaidUnits can be done with a collateral that is not activated') to realize bad debt without triggering the oracle revert, since the comment suggests this path works for non-activated collaterals? Verify whether the while loop still iterates all activated collaterals (including the reverting one) even for the (0,0) input path. Preconditions: borrower has bad debt; activated collateral j has reverting oracle; liquidator calls liquidate(collateralIndex=k, 0, 0, ...) where k is not activated. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates j (reverting oracle) and has bad debt; (2) liquidator calls liquidate(market, k, 0, 0, borrower, ...) → while loop iterates collateralBitmap including j → oracle for j reverts → revert. Invariant tested: zero-input liquidation must be executable for bad-debt realization. Scoped impact: DoS on bad-debt realization even via zero-input path. Proof idea: assert liquidate(0,0) reverts when any activated oracle reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] In the liquidate() function, the rcfThreshold check (Midnight.sol:662-667) uses _position.collateral[collateralIndex].mulDivDown(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(WAD, lif).zeroFloorSub(maxRepaid) < market.rcfThreshold. If liquidatedCollatPrice is very large (oracle returns near-max value), does this computation overflow, and can a market creator set rcfThreshold to 0 combined with an inflated oracle to bypass the RCF check entirely, allowing full liquidation of a position that should only be partially liquidatable? Preconditions: oracle returns large price; rcfThreshold = 0; position is unhealthy but RCF should limit liquidation. Call sequence: (1) oracle returns large price; (2) rcfThreshold = 0; (3) liquidator calls liquidate(repaidUnits > maxRepaid) → RCF check: zeroFloorSub(...) < 0 → always true → RCF bypassed. Invariant tested: RCF must limit liquidation amount correctly. Scoped impact: RCF bypass via oracle price inflation and zero rcfThreshold. Proof idea: assert liquidate with repaidUnits > maxRepaid reverts when rcfThreshold > 0 but succeeds when rcfThreshold = 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Does the take() function call isHealthy(offer.market, id, seller) using the market struct from the offer (not from storage), meaning that a taker can pass a crafted offer.market with a different oracle address for the same market ID, causing isHealthy to call a different oracle than the one used in liquidate()? Verify that the market ID is derived from the full market struct including oracle addresses, so a different oracle would produce a different market ID. Preconditions: taker crafts offer with modified CollateralParams.oracle; market ID check in touchMarket. Call sequence: (1) taker crafts offer with modified oracle; (2) taker calls take() → touchMarket(offer.market) → if market ID matches existing market, uses existing state; (3) isHealthy called with crafted market struct → different oracle called. Invariant tested: oracle used in health check must match oracle used in liquidation for the same market. Scoped impact: oracle substitution in health check via crafted offer. Proof idea: assert toId(market_with_different_oracle) != toId(original_market).

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower in a market with LLTV=0.98e18 (LLTV_7, near-1 value) and gamma=0.5 (LIQUIDATION_CURSOR_HIGH) exploit the fact that maxLif = WAD / (WAD - 0.5 * (WAD - 0.98e18)) ≈ 1.0101e18 is very close to 1, meaning the oracle price needs to be very precise to determine whether the RCF applies, and a small oracle manipulation (e.g., returning price ± 1 wei) can toggle the RCF condition, causing liquidate() to revert with RecoveryCloseFactorConditionsViolated when the liquidator expects it to pass? Preconditions: LLTV=0.98e18; oracle returns price at RCF boundary ± 1 wei; liquidator computes maxRepaid off-chain using slightly different price. Call sequence: (1) oracle returns P ± 1; (2) liquidator computes maxRepaid using P; (3) liquidator calls liquidate(repaidUnits = maxRepaid) → on-chain maxRepaid differs by 1 → RecoveryCloseFactorConditionsViolated. Invariant tested: RCF boundary must be stable under 1-wei oracle price changes. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via oracle price boundary manipulation. Proof idea: fuzz oracle price ± 1 around RCF boundary, assert liquidate behavior is consistent.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Does the liquidate() function's badDebt computation (Midnight.sol:614-616) use zeroFloorSub to prevent underflow, meaning that if the oracle returns a very high price making the collateral value exceed the debt, badDebt becomes 0 and no bad debt is realized? Can a market creator deploy an oracle that returns inflated prices specifically during liquidation to prevent bad-debt realization and lossFactor updates, keeping lenders' credit artificially high while the protocol is actually insolvent? Preconditions: oracle returns inflated price during liquidation; true collateral value < debt (bad debt exists); oracle makes collateral appear to cover debt. Call sequence: (1) oracle returns inflated P'; (2) badDebt = originalDebt.zeroFloorSub(collateral * P' / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE / maxLif) = 0; (3) lossFactor not updated; (4) lenders' credit not slashed; (5) protocol is insolvent but accounting shows solvency. Invariant tested: bad debt must be realized when collateral truly cannot cover debt. Scoped impact: insolvency concealment via oracle price inflation. Proof idea: assert badDebt=0 when oracle inflates price, verify protocol solvency invariant.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Can a taker exploit the fact that isHealthy is called AFTER state updates in take() (Midnight.sol:408-418 update positions, then 476 checks health) to create a scenario where the oracle price change between the state update and the health check (e.g., via oracle reentrancy or a price that changes based on Midnight's state) causes the health check to use a different price than what was used to compute the trade, resulting in a false health result? Preconditions: oracle reads Midnight state (e.g., totalUnits or withdrawable) to compute price; state changes during take affect oracle output. Call sequence: (1) oracle computes price based on Midnight.totalUnits; (2) take() updates totalUnits (line 416-417); (3) isHealthy called after update → oracle reads new totalUnits → different price → health check uses post-update price. Invariant tested: health check price must be consistent with trade execution price. Scoped impact: false health result via state-dependent oracle. Proof idea: deploy StateDependentOracle, assert isHealthy uses consistent price.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] In the liquidate() while loop, the msb() function (UtilsLib.sol:54-58) uses the clz opcode to find the highest set bit. If the collateralBitmap has bit 127 set (highest possible index) and the oracle for collateral 127 reverts, does this cause the very first iteration of the while loop to revert, blocking all liquidation regardless of which collateralIndex the liquidator targets? Verify that the msb-based iteration order (highest bit first) means a reverting high-index oracle blocks all subsequent oracle calls. Preconditions: borrower has collateral 127 activated with reverting oracle; borrower also has collateral 0 activated with healthy oracle; liquidator targets collateral 0. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates collateral 127 (reverting) and 0 (healthy); (2) liquidator calls liquidate(collateralIndex=0) → msb(bitmap) = 127 → oracle for 127 reverts → revert before reaching collateral 0. Invariant tested: liquidation of healthy-oracle collateral must not be blocked by high-index reverting oracle. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via high-index reverting oracle. Proof idea: assert liquidate(collateralIndex=0) reverts when collateral 127 oracle reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower use the authorized operator mechanism (isAuthorized mapping, Midnight.sol:192) to have an operator call supplyCollateral on their behalf to activate a collateral with a reverting oracle, and then the borrower revokes the operator's authorization, leaving the position permanently unliquidatable with no way to deactivate the reverting collateral (since withdrawCollateral with debt calls isHealthy which calls oracle)? Preconditions: borrower authorizes operator; operator calls supplyCollateral(market, j, 1, borrower) activating reverting oracle collateral; borrower revokes operator; borrower has debt. Call sequence: (1) borrower authorizes operator; (2) operator calls supplyCollateral(j, 1, borrower); (3) borrower revokes operator; (4) borrower cannot call withdrawCollateral(j, ...) because isHealthy reverts; (5) liquidator cannot call liquidate() because oracle reverts. Invariant tested: authorized operator must not be able to permanently trap borrower's position. Scoped impact: permanent position freeze via authorized operator activating reverting oracle. Proof idea: assert no path exists to deactivate reverting oracle collateral once activated with debt.
